reasoning: The sink at wf-flyout.php line 154 uses sanitize_text_field() instead of esc_attr() for the dashicons icon class inserted into an HTML class attribute. While sanitize_text_field() does not encode double-quotes and is technically wrong for an attribute context, the source of $item['icon'] is the hardcoded static config.php file shipped with the plugin (values like 'wp-captcha.png', 'dashicons-universal-access'). There is no code path by which an attacker or even an admin can influence this value at runtime: it is not read from get_option(), $_{GET,POST,REQUEST}, user meta, or any other dynamic source. The value is a compile-time constant in the plugin's own distributed source. Additionally, the entire $out string is passed through WPCaptcha_Utility::wp_kses_wf() before echoing, which calls wp_kses() with an allowlist that only permits class, style, id, data-*, and aria-hidden on <span> tags — stripping any injected attributes even if a double-quote escape were possible. The finding is a code-quality observation (wrong escaping function for context) but is not a security vulnerability because no attacker-controlled data reaches the sink.
